figures.gloss: Changing Woman — found as an infant on a mountaintop, she grows to maturity in four days, ages through the seasons and renews herself endlessly young; mother of the Hero Twins, she shaped the first Diné clans from skin rubbed from her own body and gave the Blessingway, the rite that walks a person back into hózhó, beauty-and-harmony.
figures.facet: A great mother who is herself the year: not a fertile womb-of-earth alone but a single body that grows old and young again in cycle — the great-mother and the wheel of ages fused into one ageing-and-renewing person, regeneration made biographical.
figures.provenance: Recorded from Blessingway and creation-cycle tradition (Wyman, Reichard, Zolbrod); ceremonial detail is held sacred and many tellings are deliberately incomplete in print at Diné direction.

figures.gloss: Elder of the Hero Twins, son of Changing Woman and the Sun — guided by Spider Woman past the rolling rocks and crushing reeds to his father's house, armed with lightning arrows, he hunted down the Naayééʼ, the monsters born of disorder, beheading Yéʼiitsoh the Big Giant whose blood became the black lava beds of the Southwest.
figures.facet: The slayer who pays for the killing: having cleansed the world of monsters, the twin himself sickens from the death he has dealt and must be sung back to balance by the Enemyway — violence against chaos is itself a wound that ceremony must heal.
figures.provenance: From the Diné Bahaneʼ creation cycle and the Enemyway/Monsterway ceremonials; lava fields, mountains and canyons are named as the monsters' remains across the actual Navajo landscape.

figures.gloss: Younger of the Hero Twins, conceived of his mother by dripping water as his brother was by the Sun; he does not strike the monsters but keeps the watch — holding the life-token and guarding the home-fire so that if his brother falls he will know and act, the still partner of the warrior.
figures.facet: The twin who waits: where one brother is the sword, Born-for-Water is the tether to life and home, paired guardian rather than rival — divine-twinship as warrior-and-watcher, not the two equal slayers of the Maya pattern.
figures.provenance: Held in the same Hero-Twins tradition; the water-and-sun paternity distinguishes the two and is central to how the Diné read complementary natures.

figures.gloss: The one who carries the sun — Jóhonaaʼéí bears the turquoise disk across the sky each day on his back, dwelling in a house of turquoise in the east; father of the Hero Twins, he demands they prove their lineage through deadly tests before he will arm them against the monsters.
figures.facet: A sun who is a labourer and a hard father: the solar light is a burden physically carried, and the sky-father grants his sons nothing until they survive his trials — fatherhood as a debt the children must earn, not a gift bestowed.
figures.provenance: From the Diné Bahaneʼ; the Sun's demand for payment (a life for each day's crossing) is a recurring theme in the cycle.

figures.gloss: Spider Woman, dwelling atop Spider Rock in Canyon de Chelly — she taught the Diné to weave on a loom whose crosspieces are sky and earth, sun-rays and lightning, and it is she who counsels the Hero Twins, giving the elder a feather and the words to pass his father's tests.
figures.facet: Civilisation arrives as weaving, and the weaver is the cosmos in miniature: the loom Spider Woman gives is itself a model of the quartered world, so to weave is to re-make order — the culture-hero's gift is a method for keeping hózhó thread by thread.
figures.provenance: Living tradition tied to a named landmark (Spider Rock); weaving practice and its sacred account remain active among Diné weavers.

figures.gloss: Mąʼii the Coyote, Áłtsé Hashké 'First Scolder' — the meddler who flings the carefully-placed stars into chaos across the night sky out of impatience, who steals and lies and lusts, yet whose very disorder seeds death, change and the unfixed possibility the world needs to be alive.
figures.facet: Disorder smuggled in as necessary: when Coyote scatters the ordered stars he is not merely failing — he introduces the irregular and the mortal without which the cosmos would be a sterile, frozen perfection; the trickster as the deliberate flaw that lets the world breathe.
figures.provenance: Coyote tales are traditionally told only in winter; the figure runs through both the solemn creation cycle and a vast body of comic narrative.

figures.gloss: Blanca Peak in the east, Mount Taylor in the south, the San Francisco Peaks in the west, Hesperus Peak in the north — fastened to the earth with sunbeams, lightning, a stone knife and rainbow, each dressed in its own colour (white shell, turquoise, abalone, jet); within them lies Dinétah, the proper Diné world, kept in beauty.
figures.facet: A homeland whose borders are a cosmogram: the four mountains are not merely sacred but legally definitional — to live in hózhó is to live inside this quartered, colour-coded house of the world, identity and territory and cosmic order made one bounded thing.
figures.provenance: The four peaks are real, mapped mountains still central to Diné identity and land claims; their colours and the materials binding them are standard in the tradition.

figures.gloss: First Man and First Woman, formed of ears of white and yellow corn met with wind and the medicine bundles in the lowest of the underworlds; they lead the people up through the coloured worlds to this, the Glittering World, and from First Man's bundle the mountains and the order of things are laid out.
figures.facet: Ancestors who are emergent, not made: the Diné first beings are not fashioned by a creator above but climb upward through stacked prior worlds, so origin is an ascent and a migration rather than a fabrication — a people who came up rather than being put down.
figures.provenance: From the Diné emergence (Hajíínéí) narrative; the number and colours of the underworlds vary by telling and singer.

figures.gloss: The central Diné principle — beauty, harmony, balance, rightness and health woven together: 'Sąʼah Naagháí Bikʼeh Hózhóón', the path of long life walked in beauty; every ceremony aims to restore a person or the world to hózhó, and its prayers close 'in beauty it is finished' four times over.
figures.facet: Order as a thing you walk back into, not a law you obey: hózhó is not a fixed cosmic decree but a balance constantly lost and ceremonially re-made — right-order recovered through ritual restoration, the cosmic law as ongoing repair rather than standing edict.
figures.provenance: Hózhó is the organising concept of Diné philosophy and ceremony; its full sense resists translation and the common gloss 'beauty' is acknowledged as partial.

figures.gloss: Maternal grandfather of the gods, associated with the east and the dawn, who leads the Holy People in the Nightway ceremonial; he communicates by gesture and a distinctive call rather than ordinary speech, and his mask — white face, eagle feathers, corn-ear pouch — is among the most sacred worn in Yéʼii Bicheii dances.
figures.facet: The leading figure of the Holy People speaks in the register furthest from human language — gesture and cry — making his communication itself a mark of the boundary between the diyin and Earth-Surface People.
figures.provenance: Recorded in Nightway ceremonial scholarship (Matthews, Wyman, Faris); ceremonial detail is held sacred and many tellings are deliberately incomplete in print at Diné direction.

figures.gloss: The fire god and star-maker, keeper of fire's origin and carrier of the fire drill, who places the constellations in the night sky with slow, deliberate precision — the Pleiades are said to sit upon his temple — until Coyote, impatient, flings the remaining stars into disorder across the heavens.
figures.facet: He is the craftsman half of a two-figure episode: his star-placing establishes the sky's intended order, and it is only legible as order because Coyote's interruption shows what careful placement would otherwise have produced.
figures.provenance: From the Diné Bahaneʼ creation cycle and Nightway tradition; the star-scattering episode is a standard companion piece to Coyote's own gloss in this roster.

figures.gloss: The class of beings — prior to and distinct from Earth-Surface People — who established the ceremonial order during and after the emergence, and who are embodied in ritual by masked dancers during the Yéʼii Bicheii dances of the Nightway; individual Holy People (Talking God, Black God, Changing Woman, Spider Woman among them) are addressed by name in specific ceremonies.
figures.facet: The category is prior to its members: Diné ceremony treats 'the Holy People' as the addressed class from which any individual diyin is drawn, so the masked dance embodies the office as much as the person.
figures.provenance: Nightway ceremonial scholarship (Matthews, Wyman, Faris, Reichard); the class/individual distinction is standard in the ethnographic literature.

figures.gloss: The largest of the Naayééʼ, monsters born of the disorder that followed emergence; he is Monster Slayer's chief kill in the Diné Bahaneʼ, beheaded near Mount Taylor, his congealed blood named as the black lava flows (the malpais) of the Southwestern landscape.
figures.facet: His death, not his life, is the point of the record: the actual lava fields of the region are read as his body, making the local landscape a standing, visitable proof of the monster-slaying cycle.
figures.provenance: From the Diné Bahaneʼ creation cycle and Enemyway/Monsterway ceremonials; the malpais lava beds are named as his blood across Navajo oral geography.

figures.gloss: The corpus of sung, multi-night ceremonials that restore hózhó: Hózhǫ́ǫ́jí (Blessingway), the backbone rite Changing Woman gave for blessing, protection, and life-cycle events; Enemyway, which cures the warrior's sickness brought on by killing (as Monster Slayer himself required); and Nightway, which heals through the embodied Holy People of the Yéʼii Bicheii.
figures.facet: Each named ceremonial targets a specific kind of disorder — blessing, war-sickness, illness by contact with the diyin — so the 'system' is less a single liturgy than a differentiated pharmacy of restorations, each keyed to how balance was lost.
figures.provenance: Standard in Diné ceremonial ethnography (Wyman, Reichard, Kluckhohn, Faris); specific ceremonial content is held sacred and often deliberately withheld or generalized in print at Diné direction.

figures.gloss: The wind that gives breath, thought, and guidance — entering a person at birth, said to stand visible in the whorls of the fingertips and the pattern of the ear, the medium through which the Holy People communicate with and guide human beings.
figures.facet: Wind is not merely weather but the connective medium of personhood itself: to have breath, thought, and even one's own fingerprints is already to carry a portion of Holy Wind, collapsing the gap between cosmology and physiology.
figures.provenance: James Kale McNeley, 'Holy Wind in Navajo Philosophy' (1981).

figures.gloss: A goddess associated with white shell and the east/dawn; tellings of her relation to Changing Woman vary across singers and sources — in some she is Changing Woman's sister, in others simply another of Changing Woman's own names for a different aspect or moment. The corpus does not resolve this and neither does this record.
figures.facet: The unresolved variance is itself doctrinally significant: Diné ceremonial knowledge is held by singers whose tellings differ by right, not by error, and White Shell Woman's uncertain relation to Changing Woman is a clean example of that legitimate multiplicity.
figures.provenance: Recorded across Blessingway and creation-cycle tellings (Wyman, Reichard, Zolbrod); the sister/aspect variance is itself noted in the scholarly literature rather than resolved by it.

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The principle of aesthetic, geometric, and harmonic balance that must be constantly restored through sung ritual to cure illness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Supreme female figures deeply associated with spider iconography, spinning the literal threads of fate and divine intercession.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A holistic concept of beauty, balance, and order that dictates not just moral behavior, but the exact physical and architectural orientation of daily life.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are warrior saviors tasked with returning to the earth to violently slaughter the unnatural monsters (or systemic oppressors) that prey on the people.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Specific, inviolable physical geography that serves as the anchoring axis for the entire cosmological and spiritual world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are deeply tied to the cyclic renewal of the earth, agricultural sustenance, and the origin of their respective peoples.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are manifestations of the Coyote trickster, though Snilemun functions specifically as a celestial advocate rather than a purely disruptive force.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both function as mythic grandmother figures who bestow crucial esoteric knowledge, magical aids, and rites of passage to mortals.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An entity synonymous with witchcraft, inverted social norms, and the malicious disruption of communal harmony.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The quintessential trickster-creator who is both a sacred shaper of reality and a base, foolish animal driven by bodily urges.
GOLD SEAM: The state of universal harmony, balance, and beauty that is constantly threatened by human error and must be actively restored through precise ritual action.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both seek a state of alignment with the innate, harmonious structure of the world, rather than appeasing a temperamental deity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primary feminine principle defining immediate earthly sustenance and the cyclic, domestic fertility of the people.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a divine feminine matrix inextricably linked to the cyclic aging process of nature, constantly renewing themselves without a male creator.
GOLD SEAM: Ritually establishing an inviolable, sacred center by energetically anchoring the four cardinal directions.

Two North-American emergence-and-making cosmogonies set side by side — the Iroquois earth dived up from beneath the water against the Navajo First Man and First Woman climbing up through the worlds; both build the lived world from a below, not a sky-command.

The good twin who fits the world for human life beside the Navajo Monster-Slayer who clears it of menace — paired heroic sons in two unrelated Native cosmologies, each making the world habitable, one by shaping and one by slaying.

Both are the second of a creative twin-pair, but the rhyme is loose and even inverted: Tó Bájísh Chíní is Born-for-Water, the water-twin who aids his brother, where Tawiskaron is Flint, the brother's opponent — paired-twin structure shared, moral valence reversed; flagged.

Two Native great-mothers from whose persons the people and their sustenance descend — Sky Woman who danced the island wide and bore the human line, Changing Woman who made the first Navajo clans; the ancestress as the source of the nation in two cosmologies.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an underlying cosmic and moral order that humans must actively maintain through correct speech, ritual, and harmonious living.

Both name a state of rightness one must hold to be in order — but Navajo hózhó is harmony/beauty restored through ceremony where ọfọ na ọgụ is forensic innocence in oath and law. A loose cosmic-order echo, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A system of maintaining cosmic and spatial balance through strict ritual actions and taboos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Sacred topography that enforces strict geographical orientation in daily life and ritual.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme maternal figure who directly births the founding clans of the nation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Specific, awe-inspiring geological formations that serve not as mere symbols, but as the active, literal presence of the divine.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme female creator who physically forms the first human beings directly from her own substance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The warrior son of a sky deity explicitly dispatched to rid the world of monstrous oppressors.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the profound indigenous pursuit of systemic harmony and dynamic balance in civic and spiritual life, rather than a binary of good and evil.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Among Navajo congregants, the Peyote Road is heavily syncretized with the traditional indigenous pursuit of beauty, balance, and order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both establish a vital, oriented ritual geography around which spiritual communion and identity are structurally organized.
Mother and elder son: Changing Woman bears the Hero Twins to the Sun and to the dripping water, and it is to rid her world of the devouring monsters that the elder takes up the lightning arrows.

The two Hero Twins, sun-begotten slayer and water-begotten watcher — one strikes the monsters while the other holds the life-token and guards the home; warrior and keeper, a single power split in two.
Sun-Bearer and Changing Woman, father and mother of the Hero Twins; their union (and his demands of the sons) drives the whole monster-slaying cycle.
Father and son: Jóhonaaʼéí makes the twins prove their solar lineage through lethal trials before arming them with lightning against the Naayééʼ.
Spider Woman is the twins' indispensable counsellor — she gives the elder the protective feather and the words to survive the road to the Sun's house and his father's tests.
First Man lays out the four sacred mountains from the medicine bundle after the emergence, fixing the quartered Diné world that the people climbed up into.
Changing Woman gives the Blessingway, the central rite for walking a life back into hózhó; the renewing mother and the principle of restored balance are bound at the heart of Diné ceremony.
Coyote is hózhó's necessary disturbance: his star-scattering disorder is the irregularity against which balance is forever re-made — the principle and its built-in saboteur within one cosmos.
To dwell inside the four mountains is to dwell in hózhó; the quartered homeland is the spatial body of the harmony the ceremonies restore.

The Diné Hero Twins and the Maya Hunahpu and Xbalanque are the great American twin-pair — both born of a marvel, both ridding the world of devouring powers — but a structural rhyme across unrelated families, NOT a shared line.
Paired saviour-brothers who overcome monstrous adversaries together; the Diné split into striker and watcher where the Maya twins are two equal tricksters, two faces of one widespread twin-archetype.

Lightning-armed slayers of the monster that blocks the world's life — Monster Slayer with his bolt-arrows beheading Yéʼiitsoh as Indra wields the vajra against Vṛtra; the dragon-slayer template arrived at far outside Indo-European.

Champions who slay the primordial monster of chaos and from its body shape the ordered world — Marduk splits Tiamat, Monster Slayer's giant-blood becomes the lava beds; world-from-the-slain-foe, independently reached.

Two warrior sons of a self-renewing mother who are born to destroy the enemies threatening her — the Diné Monster Slayer and the Mexica solar warrior, each violence sanctified as protection of the maternal order.
Goddesses who give the loom and the craft of weaving — but Athena is an attested figure absent from this mirror; logged as a reach toward the weaver-as-civiliser pattern, not to a present node.

Culture-bringers who hand humankind a defining art and aid the heroes against hostile powers — Spider Woman's weaving and counsel against the monsters answering Prometheus's fire and craft for mortals.

Shape-shifting troublemakers whose disorder is woven into the cosmos itself — Coyote scattering the stars, Loki breeding chaos among the gods — the trickster as the mischief the world cannot do without.
Tricksters who are also the principle of unpredictability the order requires — Coyote and Èṣù both keep the world from freezing into dead perfection by introducing chance and change.
American disorder-bringers and shape-shifters whose meddling drives the world's mutability; Coyote's smaller mischief rhymes with the Smoking Mirror's cosmic disruption, a New-World trickster cast.
Both thief, deceiver and culture-bringer at once; a loose parallel of the trickster who also benefits humankind, flagged as a reach across wholly separate families.

Mothers in whom the year's turning is embodied — Changing Woman ageing and renewing with the seasons as Demeter's grief and joy drive winter and spring; the great-mother as the cycle of the year, separately conceived.

Earth-and-fertility mothers from whom the people descend — Changing Woman forms the first clans from her own skin as Gaia brings forth the lineages; the maternal source of humankind in two unrelated worlds.
A solar-linked renewing goddess at the centre of her people's ceremony and identity — a faint parallel of the life-giving female power, with the seam tied to no shared descent.
The sun as a bearer who physically carries the disk across the sky each day — Jóhonaaʼéí on his back, Helios in his chariot — the daily solar crossing as labour, independently imagined.
Solar deities who father heroic sons and serve as the sky's measure; the Sun-Bearer's demanding paternity of the twins rhymes with Sūrya siring his sons, a parallel solar fatherhood.
Sun-gods who traverse the heavens daily and stand at the head of the cosmos; Jóhonaaʼéí's turquoise crossing answers Ra's solar barque, two unconnected accounts of the sun's journey.

The quartered, colour-coded cosmos — four Diné mountains at the cardinal points, four Tezcatlipocas holding the world's corners; the same four-directional cosmogram across separate American traditions.
A sacred centre fixed by a fourfold scheme — four mountains binding Dinétah, four rivers parting from Eden; the quartered axis-mundi independently witnessed.

Both lay the cosmos out as a quartered, four-directional figure — the Diné homeland's four colour-coded peaks and the cross-shaped Patolli board — the same diagram of the world in landscape and in play.
Sacred mountains as the structuring centre of the cosmos; the four Diné peaks bound the proper world as Meru axes the Vedic one — the world-mountain motif, four-fold here, central there.

Hózhó and Ma'at name the same office — the right-order of beauty, truth and balance the world must be kept in — but where Ma'at is a standing cosmic law, hózhó is a balance ritually re-made; one of the great cross-family rhymes of the cosmic-order joint. (Ma'at not yet a node in this mirror; seam logged toward the principle.)

Both are the all-embracing way of harmony in which one walks rather than a god to be obeyed — hózhó the path of beauty, the Dao the path of nature; cosmic order as a way of life, separately arrived at.

Hózhó belongs with Ṛta and Aṣ̌a in the family of impersonal right-orders the cosmos obeys — though uniquely it is recovered by ceremony rather than upheld by gods. (Seam logged toward the cosmic-order joint shared across the mirror.)

First beings from whom the people descend, but the Diné pair emerge by ascent through prior worlds rather than being born of the earth; a divergent parallel of the first-ancestor pattern, flagged as a reach.

Where the Sky-and-Earth world-parents must be forced apart to make the lived world, the Diné First Man and First Woman are a paired human origin reached by climbing upward through stacked worlds — emergence inverting separation as the shape of beginning.

The quieter, water-and-death-shadowed half of a divine pair — Born-for-Water as watcher beside the slayer, Xolotl the underworld twin of Quetzalcoatl; a loose parallel of the second, darker twin, flagged as a reach.
GOLD SEAM: Both equate spiritual alignment with tangible, harmonious conditions in the physical world (health, beauty, success), rejecting the idea that spirituality requires physical suffering.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both frame reality as an intricate system of balance, where disrupting one strand violently disrupts the harmony of the whole.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme divine woman who creates the people and dictates their foundational rituals, sustenance, and architectural dwellings.

The Pueblo and the neighbouring Diné share the Southwest emergence pattern — the people climb up through stacked coloured/dark lower worlds into this world; close geographic neighbours whose cosmogonies rhyme strongly, attested as a regional form rather than proven common descent.

Hopi/Zuni Spider Grandmother and Diné Spider Woman are the same Southwest weaver-counsellor — a spider grandmother atop the world who guides the people and advises the war/hero twins; neighbouring traditions sharing one figure, kept tier-2 as a regional analogue, not a demonstrated single origin.
The Pueblo war-twins and the Diné Monster Slayer are the Southwest's monster-killing sons of the Sun, counselled by Spider Grandmother/Woman; neighbouring versions of one regional war-twin tradition.
Paired Southwest war-brothers set at the world's points and born of the Sun; the Hopi Pöqángwhoya/Palöngawhoya beside the Diné Monster Slayer and Born-for-Water, the same neighbouring twin-pattern.
Hopi Sun Father and Diné Sun-Bearer are the Southwest's solar father of the twins, greeted at dawn and reckoned the measure of the day; neighbouring solar fatherhoods of one regional cast.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The literal geological features of the landscape venerated as the anchoring spiritual and territorial guardians of the indigenous nation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Specific geological formations that do not merely represent the divine, but act as literal, active conduits for divine interaction.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Right living and absolute balance recognized as both a prerequisite for and a result of spiritual alignment.

Origin as ascent into this world rather than fabrication from above — the Taíno emerge from a cave as the Diné climb through stacked prior worlds; the emergence motif independently reached across the Americas.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A state of profound, active harmony, beauty, and health that must be vigilantly maintained through collective ritual and right living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ultimate state of peace, balance, and blessing, maintained through correct action and ritual.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The conceptualization of the universe as a delicate web of relations, where moral action is defined strictly by restoring balance and harmony to the whole.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The dutiful, weapon-wielding hero who systematically cleanses the earth of alien, chaos-bringing monsters to make it habitable for humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The dynamic, encompassing state of harmony, beauty, and right relationship between humanity, nature, and the divine.
Two leading figures of the Holy People, both central to the Nightway ceremonial.
He leads the class in the Nightway; the individual diyin drawn from the general category.
One of the named Holy People embodied in the Yéʼii Bicheii dances.
Black God's deliberate, precise star-placing is what Coyote's impatient scattering interrupts — the same episode read from its two sides.
The chief monster of disorder and the hero who beheads him, named directly in Monster Slayer's own gloss.
She gave the Blessingway, the backbone rite of the Chantway system.
The Enemyway heals him of the war-sickness his own monster-slaying incurred, per his own facet.
The ceremonial mechanism by which hózhó is restored.
Wind meets the corn ears from which First Man and First Woman are formed, in the emergence account.
Holy Wind is the medium connecting Holy People and humans; hózhó is the balance that connection is meant to sustain.
Widely told as sisters, sometimes as one figure's two names — the tradition itself holds the relation open; kept at the lower, structural-analogy tier rather than claimed as settled kinship.
Fire-wielding divine craftsmen whose deliberate work — forge or star-placing — imposes precise order on raw material, independently conceived.
Impersonal vital forces entering and animating persons and enabling contact with the sacred, in two unrelated North American frameworks.
Breath as the literal medium connecting the divine and the human, arrived at independently in wholly separate traditions.

dossier.essence: The traditional religion of the Diné (Navajo), the largest Native nation in the United States, centered on their homeland (Dinétah) in the Four Corners region of the Southwest. Its governing aim is hózhó — a state of beauty, balance, order, and health — continually lost and ceremonially restored, and its central figures are the Holy People (Diyin Dineʼé), above all Changing Woman and her sons the Hero Twins.
dossier.cosmos: The Diné cosmos is a stack of prior worlds up through which First Man, First Woman, and the ancestral beings emerged into this, the Glittering World, whose bounds were fixed as four sacred mountains (Blanca Peak, Mount Taylor, the San Francisco Peaks, Hesperus Peak). Changing Woman, found on a mountaintop and endlessly renewing herself, made the first clans and gave the Blessingway; her sons, born to the Sun, cleared the world of the monsters (Naayééʼ) born of disorder, yet the very act of killing left them sick and needing ceremonial cure. The stress falls not on afterlife — the dead and their ghosts (chʼį́įdii) are treated with caution and avoidance — but on maintaining hózhó among the living.
dossier.practice: Religion is enacted through 'chantway' ceremonies conducted by trained singers (hataałii) who must recite long song-prayer sequences and lay drypaintings ('sandpaintings') with exact accuracy to restore a patient to hózhó — the Blessingway (Hózhóójí) for good fortune and life-passage, the Enemyway for the sickness of contact with death and outsiders, and curing chants such as Nightway. Knowledge is specialized, orally transmitted, and partly restricted; certain stories, notably the Coyote tales, are told only in winter. Much ceremonial detail is deliberately withheld from publication at Diné direction.
dossier.canon: An oral corpus — the Diné Bahaneʼ (emergence and creation story), the Blessingway, and the chantway myths and songs — with no scripture; the standard printed versions (Washington Matthews, Gladys Reichard, Leland Wyman, Paul Zolbrod) are partial recordings of living, restricted tradition.
dossier.reflects: Order as ongoing repair — the sacred not as a law once handed down but as a harmony forever lost to disorder and re-made, thread by thread, through precise ceremony.
sources: Gladys A. Reichard, 'Navaho Religion: A Study of Symbolism' (1950)
Paul G. Zolbrod, 'Diné bahaneʼ: The Navajo Creation Story' (1984)
